94:    ```
95: 
96: ## Environment Variables
97: 
98: ### Local Development
99: 
100: Create a `.env` file (ignored by git) for local development:
101: 
102: ```bash
103: # .env (DO NOT commit this file)
104: 
105: # Add your project-specific environment variables here
106: # Examples:
107: 
108: # Database connection
109: # DATABASE_URL=postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb
110: 
111: # API keys (use dummy values for local dev)
112: # API_KEY=dev-key-12345
113: 
114: # Feature flags
115: # ENABLE_FEATURE_X=true
116: ```
117: 
118: ### Best Practices
119: 
120: - **Never commit secrets** to version control
121: - Use `.env` files for local development (add to `.gitignore`)
122: - Use environment-specific configurations (dev, staging, prod)
123: - Document all required environment variables in this file
124: - Provide example values in `.env.example` (committed to repo)
125: 
126: ### Secret Scanning
127: 
128: - This repository uses **Gitleaks** (via pre-commit) to prevent credentials from being committed. Gitleaks was selected over TruffleHog and Talisman because it has a larger contributor base, frequent releases (see [Gitleaks release notes](https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks/releases)), and first-class pre-commit support.
129: - Hooks run automatically when you execute `pre-commit install`, but you can run an ad-hoc scan with:
130: 
131:   ```bash
132:   pre-commit run gitleaks --all-files
133:   ```
134: 
135: - In CI or manual audits you can also use the standalone CLI:
136: 
137:   ```bash
138:   gitleaks detect --redact --report-format sarif --report-path reports/gitleaks.sarif
139:   ```
140: 
141: - If the hook blocks a commit, remove the offending secret, rotate the credential, and re-run the scan.
142: 
143: ### Required Environment Variables
144: 
145: <!-- Document your project's required environment variables here -->
146: 
147: | Variable | Description | Required | Default |
148: | ---------- | ------------- | ---------- | --------- |
149: | `DATABASE_URL` | Database connection string | Yes (prod) | `sqlite:///local.db` (dev) |
150: | `API_KEY` | External API key | Yes (prod) | `dev-key` (dev) |
151: | `LOG_LEVEL` | Logging verbosity | No | `INFO` |
152: 
153: ## Testing and QA
154: 
155: ### Running Tests
156: 
157: ```bash
158: # Add your project's test commands here
159: # Examples:
160: 
161: # Unit tests
162: # npm test                    # Node.js
163: # pytest tests/unit           # Python
164: # go test ./...               # Go
165: # cargo test                  # Rust
166: 
167: # Integration tests
168: # npm run test:integration    # Node.js
169: # pytest tests/integration    # Python
170: 
171: # End-to-end tests
172: # npm run test:e2e            # Node.js
173: # pytest tests/e2e            # Python
174: 
175: # With coverage
176: # npm test -- --coverage      # Node.js
177: # pytest --cov=src tests/     # Python
178: # go test -cover ./...        # Go
179: # cargo tarpaulin             # Rust
180: ```
181: 
182: ### Quality Checks
183: 
184: ```bash
185: # Run linters
186: # npm run lint                # Node.js
187: # ruff check .                # Python
188: # golangci-lint run           # Go
189: # cargo clippy                # Rust
190: 
191: # Run formatters
192: # npm run format              # Node.js
193: # ruff format .               # Python
194: # gofmt -w .                  # Go
195: # cargo fmt                   # Rust
196: 
197: # Run all pre-commit hooks
198: pre-commit run --all-files
199: ```
200: 
201: ### Testing Guidelines
202: 
203: - Write tests for all new features and bug fixes
204: - Maintain test coverage above 80%
205: - Use descriptive test names that explain intent
206: - Follow the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern
207: - Mock external dependencies in unit tests
208: - Use integration tests for testing component interactions
209: - Include end-to-end tests for critical user flows
210: 
211: ### Test Organization
212: 
213: ```text
214: tests/
215: ├── unit/           # Fast, isolated unit tests
216: ├── integration/    # Tests with database, API calls, etc.
217: ├── e2e/           # Full application workflow tests
218: └── fixtures/      # Test data and mocks
219: ```
220: 
221: ## Project-Specific Customizations
222: 
223: <!-- Add project-specific development guidance here -->
224: 
225: ### Custom Scripts
226:

228: # Add project-specific scripts and commands here
229: # Examples:
230:

301: 
302: ## Troubleshooting
303: 
304: ### Common Issues
305: 
306: #### Pre-commit hooks fail
307: 
308: ```bash
309: # Update pre-commit hooks
310: pre-commit autoupdate
311: 
312: # Run hooks manually
313: pre-commit run --all-files
314: 
315: # Skip hooks temporarily (not recommended)
316: SKIP=hook-name git commit -m "message"
317: ```
318: 
319: #### CI fails but tests pass locally
320: 
321: - Ensure all dependencies are locked (package-lock.json, poetry.lock, etc.)
322: - Check environment variables in CI configuration
323: - Review CI logs for differences from local environment
324: 
325: #### Merge conflicts
326: 
327: ```bash
328: # Update your branch with latest main
329: git fetch origin
330: git rebase origin/main
331: 
332: # Resolve conflicts and continue
333: git rebase --continue
334: ```
335: 
336: ## Additional Resources
337: 
338: - [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md) - Contribution guidelines
339: - [Template Customization Guide](template-guide.md) - Repository settings and branch protection
340: - [README.md](../README.md) - Project overview and quick start
341: - [GitHub Actions Documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/actions)
342: - [pre-commit Documentation](https://pre-commit.com/)
343: - [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)

(End of file - total 343 lines)
</content>

<content>1: # Template Customization Guide
2: 
3: This guide provides detailed instructions for customizing this base template for your project.
4: 
5: ## Customization Checklist
6: 
7: After creating your repository from this template, follow the checklist below:
8: 
9: ### Repository Configuration
10: 
11: - [ ] Update repository name and description in GitHub settings
12: - [ ] Update `README.md` with your project name, description, and badges
13: - [ ] Update `.github/chainguard/main-semantic-release.sts.yaml` with your repository path
14: - [ ] Review and customize `.gitignore` for your language/framework
15: 
16: ### CI/CD Workflows
17: 
18: - [ ] Update `.github/workflows/ci.yml` workflow name if needed
19: - [ ] Add language-specific setup steps (Node.js, Python, Go, Rust, Java, etc.)
20: - [ ] Replace placeholder test and lint commands with your actual commands
21: - [ ] Update paths-ignore in workflows if needed
22: - [ ] Review `.github/workflows/release.yml` and customize build/release steps
23: 
24: ### Semantic Release Setup (First Release)
25: 
26: If you're using python-semantic-release (PSR), start your project at an initial version of `0.0.0`.
27: 
28: Why: PSR only commits files that *change* during version stamping. If you start at your "first real"
29: version (e.g. `0.1.0`), then the release commit may not include your version file(s) or lockfile(s)
30: (because PSR has nothing to update), which is confusing during initial setup.
31: 
32: Examples:
33: 
34: - Python (`pyproject.toml`): set `project.version = "0.0.0"` and configure PSR `version_toml`.
35: - uv (`uv.lock`): run `uv lock` after stamping so the lockfile reflects the stamped version.
36: 
37: In practice:
38: 
39: - [ ] Set your package metadata version to `0.0.0`
40: - [ ] Configure PSR to stamp the version into that file
41: - [ ] Ensure your release process regenerates lockfiles (if applicable) so they get committed
42: 
43: #### Version Bumping Behavior (`major_on_zero = false`)
44: 
45: This template configures `major_on_zero = false` in `.releaserc.toml`. This means:
46: 
47: - **Breaking changes in 0.x.x versions** will bump the **minor** version (0.0.0 → 0.1.0)
48: - **Breaking changes in 1.x.x+ versions** will bump the **major** version (1.0.0 → 2.0.0)
49: 
50: **Important:** With this configuration, **v1.0.0 releases must be explicitly and manually created**. When you're ready for your first stable release:
51: 
52: 1. Temporarily set `major_on_zero = true` in `.releaserc.toml`, OR
53: 2. Use the `--major` flag: `semantic-release version --major`
54: 
55: After creating v1.0.0, you can revert `major_on_zero` back to `false` if desired (future breaking changes will then bump to 2.0.0, 3.0.0, etc.).
56: 
57: > **Best Practice:** Always pin GitHub Actions to specific versions (e.g., `@v4`) for stability and security. Avoid `@latest` except for rapidly evolving actions where you commit to active monitoring.
58: 
59: ### Pre-commit Hooks
60: 
61: - [ ] Review `.pre-commit-config.yaml`: add language-specific hooks and remove any that are not needed.
62: - [ ] Configure hook versions to match your project's requirements
63: - [ ] Run `pre-commit autoupdate` to update the hooks to the latest versions.
64: 
65: ### Dependency Management
66: 
67: - [ ] Verify Renovate Bot is installed for your organization (if using dependency management)
68: - [ ] Review `.github/renovate.json` and customize it for your project.
69: - [ ] Run `npx --yes --package renovate -- renovate-config-validator` to validate the configuration.
70: - [ ] Review the initial onboarding PR and merge it if everything looks good.
71: 
72: ### Documentation
73: 
74: - [ ] Update `README.md` with project-specific information
75: - [ ] Customize `CONTRIBUTING.md` with project-specific contribution guidelines
76: - [ ] Review and update issue templates in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`
77: - [ ] Customize pull request template in `.github/pull_request_template.md`
78: - [ ] Update `docs/development.md` with project-specific setup instructions
79: - [ ] Update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` with your system architecture
80: - [ ] Update `AGENTS.md` with any specific instructions for AI assistants not covered by the other documentation.
81: - [ ] Remove any placeholders like `{OWNER}`, `{REPO}`, `{ORGANIZATION}`, `{TEAM_NAME}`, etc.
82: - [ ] Delete the CHANGELOG.md file if it exists. Semantic release will generate it automatically.
83: - [ ] Delete this file.
84: 
85: ### Secrets and Authentication
86: 
87: The following may need to be configured at your organization level:
88: 
89: - [ ] Verify Octo STS is configured (for semantic-release workflow) - if using Chainguard
90: - [ ] Verify Renovate Bot GitHub App is installed (if `.github/renovate.json` exists)
91: 
92: ### Repository Settings
93: 
94: - [ ] Apply general repository settings (see commands below)
95: - [ ] Enable branch protection for `main` branch using `.github/ruleset-config.json`
96: - [ ] Add Chainguard Octo STS to bypass list (if using semantic-release)
97: - [ ] Configure required status checks (CI workflows)
98: - [ ] Set up CODEOWNERS file if needed
99: 
100: **Apply settings via GitHub CLI:**
101: 
102: ```bash
103: # General settings (squash merge only, auto-delete branches)
104: gh api -X PATCH repos/{owner}/{repo} \
105:   -F has_issues=true \
106:   -F has_wiki=true \
107:   -F allow_squash_merge=true \
108:   -F allow_merge_commit=false \
109:   -F allow_rebase_merge=false \
110:   -F delete_branch_on_merge=true
111: 
112: # Branch protection ruleset
113: gh api -X POST repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets \
114:   --input .github/ruleset-config.json
115: ```
116: 
117: **Important:** If using semantic-release, add Chainguard Octo STS to the ruleset bypass list via GitHub UI: Settings → Rules → Rulesets → Edit → Bypass list → Add "Chainguard Octo-sts".
118: 
119: ### Post-Customization Verification
120: 
121: After completing customization:
122: 
123: 1. Run pre-commit hooks to verify all files pass: `pre-commit run --all-files`
124: 2. Review branch protection settings are correctly configured
125: 3. Create a conventional commit and push it to the main branch
126: 4. Verify CI workflows pass on the main branch
127: 5. Verify the semantic release workflow runs successfully and creates a new release

(End of file - total 127 lines)
</content>

<content>1: name: Semantic Release
2: 
3: # This workflow runs after CI passes on the main branch.
4: # It analyzes commits using Conventional Commits and automatically:
5: # - Bumps the version
6: # - Generates/updates CHANGELOG.md
7: # - Creates a GitHub release
8: 
9: on:
10:   workflow_run:
11:     # Update this to match your CI workflow name (default: "CI - Tests and Linting")
12:     workflows: ["CI - Tests and Linting"]
13:     branches: [main]
14:     types: [completed]
15: 
16: permissions:
17:   contents: write
18:   issues: write
19:   pull-requests: write
20: 
21: jobs:
22:   semantic-release:
23:     if: ${{ github.event.workflow_run.conclusion == 'success' }}
24:     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
25:     permissions:
26:       contents: read  # Required for actions/checkout
27:       id-token: write  # Required for Octo STS
28:     concurrency:
29:       group: semantic-release-${{ github.ref }}
30:       cancel-in-progress: false
31:     steps:
32:       # Chainguard Octo STS authentication
33:       # This provides short-lived tokens for GitHub Actions without storing long-lived secrets.
34:       # Configured at Liatrio org level. For external adoption:
35:       #   1. Set up Octo STS: https://github.com/octo-sts/app
36:       #   2. Configure identity in .github/chainguard/main-semantic-release.sts.yaml
37:       #   3. Add the Octo STS GitHub App to your repository's ruleset bypass list
38:       # Alternative: Replace this step with a GitHub PAT or GitHub App token
39:       - uses: octo-sts/action@6177b4481c00308b3839969c3eca88c96a91775f # v1.0.0
40:         id: octo-sts
41:         with:
42:           scope: ${{ github.repository }}
43:           identity: main-semantic-release
44: 
45:       - uses: actions/checkout@v4
46:         with:
47:           fetch-depth: 0
48:           fetch-tags: true
49:           persist-credentials: false
50: 
51:       - name: Get GitHub App User ID
52:         id: get-user-id
53:         run: echo "user-id=$(gh api "/users/octo-sts[bot]" --jq .id)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
54:         env:
55:           GH_TOKEN: ${{ steps.octo-sts.outputs.token }}
56: 
57:       - name: Configure git author
58:         run: |
59:           set -eox pipefail
60:           git config --global user.name 'octo-sts[bot]'
61:           git config --global user.email '${{ steps.get-user-id.outputs.user-id }}+octo-sts[bot]@users.noreply.github.com'
62: 
63:       - name: Use PAT for pushes to origin
64:         run: |
65:           git remote set-url origin \
66:             https://x-access-token:${{ steps.octo-sts.outputs.token }}@github.com/${{ github.repository }}.git
67:           # sanity check
68:           git ls-remote --heads origin >/dev/null
69: 
70:       # TODO: Add language-specific build steps here if needed
71:       # Examples:
72:       # - Node.js: npm ci && npm run build
73:       # - Python: pip install build && python -m build
74:       # - Go: go build -v ./...
75:       # - Rust: cargo build --release
76:       # - Java: mvn package or gradle build
77: 
78:       - name: Install uv
79:         uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v7
80:         with:
81:           enable-cache: true
82: 
83:       - name: Semantic Release
84:         run: uvx python-semantic-release@v10.5.3 --verbose --config .releaserc.toml version
85:         env:
86:           GH_TOKEN: ${{ steps.octo-sts.outputs.token }}
87: 
88:       # NOTE: Best practice for first-time PSR setup
89:       #
90:       # Start your project metadata version at "0.0.0".
91:       # Why: PSR only commits files that change during version stamping. If you
92:       # start at your first intended release version (e.g. "0.1.0"), PSR may not
93:       # include your version file(s) or lockfile(s) in the first release commit
94:       # (because there's nothing to update).
95:       #
96:       # When creating your project from this template:
97:       # 1. Ensure your package metadata version is "0.0.0"
98:       # 2. Remove CHANGELOG.md if it exists (PSR will generate it)
99:       # 3. Delete any existing tags (if you already experimented): git tag -d $(git tag -l)
100:       # 4. Customize the [semantic_release] section in .releaserc.toml
101:       #    - Add `version_toml`/`version_variables` targets
102:       #    - Include lockfiles in `assets` if applicable (e.g. uv.lock)
103:       #    - Configure `build_command` to regenerate lockfiles/build artifacts
104:       #
105:       # NOTE: Version bumping behavior (major_on_zero = false)
106:       #
107:       # This template sets `major_on_zero = false`, which means breaking changes
108:       # in 0.x.x versions bump the minor version (0.0.0 -> 0.1.0), not major.
109:       # This ensures your first release will be v0.1.0 even with breaking changes.
110:       #
111:       # IMPORTANT: v1.0.0 releases must be explicitly created by:
112:       # - Temporarily setting `major_on_zero = true` in .releaserc.toml, OR
113:       # - Using `--major` flag: `semantic-release version --major`
114: 
115:       # NOTE: Build commands should be configured in .releaserc.toml (build_command), not here

<type>file</type>
<content>1: # See https://python-semantic-release.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration/configuration.html
2: # for detailed documentation on the configuration options.
3: 
4: [semantic_release]
5: # Use annotated tags like v1.2.3
6: tag_format = "v{version}"
7: # Allow 0.x.x versions (required for pre-1.0.0 releases)
8: allow_zero_version = true
9: # Breaking changes in 0.x.x should bump minor, not major (0.0.0 -> 0.1.0, not 1.0.0)
10: # This ensures the first release will be v0.1.0 even when breaking changes are present.
11: # IMPORTANT: With this setting, v1.0.0 releases must be explicitly and manually created
12: # by temporarily removing this setting or using --major flag when ready for 1.0.0.
13: major_on_zero = false
14: # Version variables: files to update with version numbers during release
15: # For projects using this template, add language-specific version files here.
16: #
17: # Best practice for initial setup: set your project version to "0.0.0" in your
18: # package metadata file (e.g. `pyproject.toml`, `package.json`, `Cargo.toml`).
19: # This ensures the *first* release commit visibly stamps version files.
20: #
21: # Examples:
22: # - Python (pyproject.toml): use TOML-aware stamping:
23: #   version_toml = ["pyproject.toml:project.version"]
24: # - Non-TOML files: use regex-based stamping:
25: #   version_variables = ["package.json:version"]
26: version_variables = []
27: 
28: # Assets to include in the release commit
29: # (Tip: include lockfiles like `uv.lock`, `package-lock.json`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`)
30: assets = []
31: # Commit message for release commits - includes [skip ci] to prevent CI from running
32: commit_message = "chore(release): {version} [skip ci]\n\nAutomatically generated by python-semantic-release"
33: 
34: # Optional: Custom build command to run during versioning
35: # (Runs after version stamping, before tagging)
36: #
37: # Example for Python projects with uv (keeps `uv.lock` synced with stamped version):
38: # build_command = "uv lock && uv build"
39: #
40: # Example for Python projects:
41: # build_command = "pip install build && python -m build"
42: # Example for Node.js projects:
43: # build_command = "npm ci && npm run build"
44: # Example for Go projects:
45: # build_command = "go build -v ./..."
46: # Example for Rust projects:
47: # build_command = "cargo build --release"
48: 
49: [semantic_release.changelog]
50: # Generate CHANGELOG.md in Markdown
51: # Use nested section syntax (required for proper parsing)
52: [semantic_release.changelog.default_templates]
53: changelog_file = "CHANGELOG.md"
54: output_format = "md"
55: 
56: [semantic_release.branches]
57: # Release from the main branch
58: main = { match = "main" }
59: 
60: [semantic_release.remote]
61: # Use GitHub token from environment variable
62: token = { env = "GH_TOKEN" }

(End of file - total 62 lines)
</content>

parts.text:
**Inspecting configuration setups**

I’m thinking about whether I need to inspect environmental examples or use glob patterns. I should search for any unsafe guidance or placeholders by using grep in the target repository, excluding .git files. I plan to check for patterns like `{OWNER}`, `{REPO}`, and others. It might also be worthwhile to review the CHANGELOG. I’ll need to evaluate the commands and their realism, especially regarding the setup of Node in the pre-commit hook and how it interacts with validators.

**Evaluating repository readiness**

I’m thinking about whether I should consider future/manual options phrased as "standalone CLI." It might be fine if it's optional and external. I need to inspect the live remote, possibly using `git remote -v` or `gh repo view`. Since the task asks for auditing repo readiness, maybe local checks will suffice, but I should summarize the live remote state. I could use the `gh api` for read-only remote audits and check settings and rulesets, especially since a governance or authentication mismatch could block a release.

**Auditing repository templates**

I need to determine if the template-repo lens applies and identify any unsafe remote apply guidance. It seems that remote governance is relevant here. I can conduct a read-only audit using `gh`, considering the user hasn't restricted remote reads, just remote changes. I might use `gh api GET` for this. I should also check if the default branch workflows are active, starting with `git remote show` or `gh repo details`. Time to dive into it!

This way, I can gather all the needed information efficiently, allowing me to see the current setup clearly.

**Considering remote reads and parsing**

I’m thinking about running `gh` for remote reads, but I've already done that. Next, I might need to run `python -c` to parse the `.releaserc.toml` file and the ruleset JSON. There’s also the option of checking the version with `pre-commit --version`, but that might not be sufficient on its own. I just want to make sure I'm covering all my bases here!

**Inspecting branch settings**

I need to check if the current branch head commit includes [skip ci] and assess the workflows. I already have the branch's commit message, so next, I’ll verify the default branch activation summary — particularly for active workflows like ci/release and extra CodeQL. My local setup should have ci/release active, but the remote version doesn't. Also, issue tracking appears to be disabled remotely, even with issue templates present. I wonder if there are contradictions in the current state documentation.

**Assessing repository settings**

I’m reviewing the README, which states I need to configure branch protection and repository settings. I noticed that AGENTS has a "Apply branch protection" step involving a raw POST command, which raises some safety concerns due to the lack of audit or approval. The CONTRIBUTING document normalizes the use of `--no-verify` and `[skip ci]`, which could present safety issues too. I'll also check for placeholders in the documentation to ensure they're relevant and not misleading for downstream customization.

**Examining template discrepancies**

In the live template repository's development guide, there's confusing guidance since it lacks a .env.example file and doesn't use environment variables. This could mislead users, making it feel like stale template residue instead of useful customization. The "Required Environment Variables" section appears updated, but it might actually misrepresent the template's intent. Similarly, the docs/ARCHITECTURE use sample diagrams that don't match the template structure. I need to assess if the README or documentation mentions any existing tests or linting to clarify their claims.

**Evaluating CI/CD documentation**

The README states that the CI/CD workflow includes placeholder tests and linting, which I understand need to be customized based on the specific programming language. That seems reasonable. Additionally, AGENTS mentions a CI workflow with language placeholders as well. It's important to ensure these placeholders are effectively guiding users on how to implement their unique setups. I wonder if further clarification is needed for both documents to make the instructions even more user-friendly.

**Evaluating repo upgrade process**

I’m considering whether to inspect adoption sources, but it feels like it might not be relevant. My main focus is determining the workflow—whether to upgrade the repo or create a new one. Since the repo has a meaningful structure, I'll probably choose the upgrade path. I need to audit the current state through the lens of a template repo. A report detailing compliant baseline items and gaps by severity seems necessary, and I'll need to gather specific line references for that.

**Considering validation details**

I need to mention any failing or skipped checks, like the skipped `pre-commit run --all-files` due to being audit-only and the mutating hooks. It's important to note there are no live rulesets and that the branch protection endpoint returned a 404 error. I should consider running JSON or TOML validation to ensure desired files can be parsed. parts.text:
**Evaluating repository governance**

I need to check if a CHANGELOG exists and if it’s properly generated. It's not essential to read it, but there might be mentions of releases and tags. I also want to inspect the repo settings. The current state of issues being disabled despite having issue templates is a governance mismatch. This may point to a medium or high governance gap, especially if there are broken URL placeholders impacting support navigation. I'll keep exploring the implications of this.

**Assessing governance and safety**

It seems there’s a significant governance issue due to automation misrouting. With broken issue contact links and disabled issues, the support flow for contributors is hindered. This feels high-risk since it's invalidating governance and navigation. I might classify the direct use of raw GitHub API commands as a medium safety concern, especially if skipping hooks is normalized, which could rank as high. It may be medium-high risk due to the guidance on unsafe remote applies. I’ll need to weigh these factors closely.

**Establishing severity levels**

For the severity assessment, I see a couple of high-risk factors. First, the `.github/CODEOWNERS` has unresolved placeholders which disrupt ownership metadata and compromise governance. This situation can break the `reviewersFromCodeOwners` functionality. Second, there’s unsafe remote apply guidance in `AGENTS.md` and `docs/template-guide.md` that presents raw `gh api` commands without proper framing for audit or approval, which could lead to direct mutation. I’m thinking this may be high risk due to the mention of unsafe guidance.

**Evaluating governance issues**

I'm considering the governance issue with the invalid contact in the `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` because it has placeholder URLs and the live repo has issues disabled. I'm thinking this might be medium to high severity, especially since broken support links are clearly invalidating governance. For the docs, the claims for environment variables or installation could mislead adopters, which is low. Also, the architecture diagrams seem generic and unrelated to the base repo template. Lastly, the AGENTS context marker doesn't provide relevant instructions.

**Noting compliant items**

I should highlight the compliant elements here. The README, CONTRIBUTING, AGENTS, and docs exist and mostly describe the template repo accurately. There’s a pre-commit config with baseline hooks, including commitlint, gitleaks, and a renovate validator. The CI workflow maintains required status checks and includes honest placeholder test/lint jobs alongside a real pre-commit job. Plus, the release workflow and `.releaserc.toml` are functional, and the desired state of the ruleset in `.github/ruleset-config.json` matches the CI job names.

**Evaluating governance improvements**

I'm thinking I need to identify minimum baseline gaps versus a stronger target state for governance. 

For the baseline, it's important to have concrete CODEOWNERS, safe audit documentation, accurate support links, and a clear repository settings state. 

On the stronger side, I want to include improvements like ruleset drift detection and machine-readable repo settings.

I also need to validate the commands run against YAML, JSON, and TOML files, ensuring everything is in order for remote reads and active workflows.

**Analyzing skipped checks and risks**

It looks like I encountered some skipped checks. The `gh api .../branches/main/protection` returned a 404 because the branch is unprotected, and I also skipped `pre-commit run --all-files` due to audit-only hooks. 

I need to consider residual risks, like template consumers possibly misapplying unsafe guidance. Plus, the live repo governance is weaker than the desired state. The success of releases relies on the unprotected branch, which complicates things. I should mention runnable commands like `pre-commit install` and `gh workflow list`, which aren't adequately documented.

*** Add File: /home/damien/Liatrio/repos/ai-prompts/skills/ai-native-repo-bootstrap-workspace/iteration-2/eval-1-template-repo-audit/with_skill/run-1/outputs/report.md
+# AI-Native Repo Readiness Audit
+
+## Repository Summary
+
+- Repository path: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
+- Repository purpose: GitHub template repo for reusable project scaffolding, quality gates, and release/governance bootstrap, evidenced in `README.md:1`, `README.md:3`, and remote `is_template=true`.
+- Current maturity profile: `docs-first` with a strong template-repo operations layer; the repo product is scaffolding, not an implemented application.
+- Template-repo lens needed: yes; downstream customization placeholders are expected in adopter-facing scaffolding, but the live template repo still needs truthful current-repo governance and safety guidance.
+- Workflow used: `upgrade_repo`
+
+## State Separation
+
+- Local desired state: versioned baseline docs plus `pre-commit`, CI, release, Renovate, and branch-governance intent in `.pre-commit-config.yaml:1`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:1`, `.github/workflows/release.yml:1`, `.github/ruleset-config.json:1`, and `.releaserc.toml:1`.
+- Repo-settings desired-state evidence: only partially captured as prose and raw `gh api` apply snippets in `docs/template-guide.md:92`; there is no first-class repo-settings file or audit table.
+- Live remote state: public GitHub template repo with releases enabled and active workflows, but with issues disabled, merge commit and rebase merge enabled, delete-branch-on-merge disabled, no live rulesets, and `main` unprotected.
+- Default-branch activation state: `CI - Tests and Linting` and `Semantic Release` are active on the default branch, but `.github/ruleset-config.json` is not activated remotely; `gh workflow list` also shows an extra active `CodeQL` workflow that is not tracked in the local repo.
+
+## Compliant Baseline Items
+
+- Repo intent is legible for a template repo in `README.md:1`, `README.md:18`, and `AGENTS.md:7`.
+- AI-operability guidance exists and accurately describes the repo as a language-agnostic template in `AGENTS.md:9` and `AGENTS.md:73`.
+- Local quality baseline is present and syntactically valid in `.pre-commit-config.yaml:1` and `.markdownlint.yaml:1`; hooks include YAML, TOML, markdown, commit message, secret, and Renovate validation.
+- CI preserves required checks with honest placeholder test/lint jobs plus a real pre-commit gate in `.github/workflows/ci.yml:12`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:48`, and `.github/workflows/ci.yml:105`.
+- Release scaffolding is present and syntactically valid in `.github/workflows/release.yml:1` and `.releaserc.toml:4`; live releases `v1.0.0` and `v1.0.1` show the current unprotected remote can complete the workflow.
+- Ruleset intent is versioned and its required check contexts match the CI job names in `.github/ruleset-config.json:30`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:13`, and `.github/workflows/ci.yml:49`.
+
+## Truthfulness And Command Realism
+
+- Current runnable commands: `pre-commit install` and `pre-commit run --all-files` are supported by `.pre-commit-config.yaml:1`; remote audit commands using `gh` are appropriate for GitHub governance inspection.
+- Future-state commands that are clearly labeled: CI placeholder install/test/lint steps are clearly called out as placeholders in `.github/workflows/ci.yml:28`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:38`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:64`, and `.github/workflows/ci.yml:69`.
+- Contradictions or unsupported commands:
+  - `AGENTS.md:61` presents a raw ruleset create call as the default next step, not an audit-first flow.
+  - `docs/template-guide.md:100` presents raw PATCH and POST apply commands before any inspect/compare/approve sequence.
+  - `docs/development.md:135` suggests `gitleaks detect` via a standalone CLI that the repo does not provision.
+  - `docs/development.md:143` through `docs/development.md:151` presents required environment variables for a repo that has no implemented runtime or `.env.example`, which overstates current-state reality.
+
+## Placeholder And Template Residue Findings
+
+- Downstream-customization placeholders:
+  - Template creation and customization placeholders in `README.md:25`, `docs/template-guide.md:7`, and `.github/workflows/ci.yml:19` are appropriate for adopters when clearly labeled.
+  - `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:3` is an explicit placeholder template and is acceptable for a template repo, though it remains generic.
+- Maturity-based placeholders:
+  - Empty release stamp targets in `.releaserc.toml:26` and language-specific commented hook examples in `.pre-commit-config.yaml:47` are acceptable docs-first/template-stage deferrals.
+- Stale template residue:
+  - `docs/development.md:147` through `docs/development.md:151` hard-code generic app environment variables that do not describe this repo.
+  - `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:23` through `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:304` models a generic app architecture instead of the template repo's own structure or an explicitly downstream-only architecture stub.
+- Governance-invalidating placeholders:
+  - `.github/CODEOWNERS:1` through `.github/CODEOWNERS:2` still use `@{ORGANIZATION}/{TEAM_NAME}`.
+  - `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:3` through `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:8` still use `{OWNER}/{REPO}` links.
+  - Governance mutation guidance in `AGENTS.md:61` and `docs/template-guide.md:100` is not fenced by audit-first or approval-gated language.
+
+## Gaps By Severity
+
+### High
+
+- Invalid live `CODEOWNERS` placeholder in `.github/CODEOWNERS:1` through `.github/CODEOWNERS:2` makes ownership non-concrete and undermines `reviewersFromCodeOwners` in `.github/renovate.json:12`.
+- Broken issue contact links in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:3` through `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:8` are governance-invalidating placeholders, and the live remote has `has_issues=false`, so contributor support/navigation is internally inconsistent.
+- Unsafe remote apply guidance is normalized in `AGENTS.md:61` through `AGENTS.md:65` and `docs/template-guide.md:100` through `docs/template-guide.md:117`; this fails the audit-first, approval-gated governance standard.
+
+### Medium
+
+- Hook-bypass guidance is normalized in `CONTRIBUTING.md:135` through `CONTRIBUTING.md:143` and `docs/development.md:315` through `docs/development.md:317`, including `--no-verify`, which is an explicit safety smell.
+- Local governance intent and live remote state materially drift: `.github/ruleset-config.json:1` is not applied, `main` is unprotected, and live repo settings differ from the intended squash-only/delete-branch guidance in `docs/template-guide.md:100` through `docs/template-guide.md:115`.
+- Release path is only viable because the live branch is currently unprotected; the versioned desired state does not encode the Octo STS bypass actor and instead relies on manual UI steps in `docs/template-guide.md:117`, so a future ruleset apply could block release pushes.
+- Repo-settings desired state is not first-class or machine-readable; it is buried in raw apply prose instead of an auditable desired-state artifact.
+
+### Low
+
+- `docs/development.md:46` through `docs/development.md:94` mixes truthful baseline setup with generic dependency/test examples that still read close to present-tense instructions.
+- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:1` is clearly marked as a placeholder, but it still reads like a generic application architecture rather than a template-repo or downstream-consumer artifact.
+
+## Minimum Baseline Vs Stronger Target State
+
+- Minimum acceptable baseline gaps:
+  - Replace governance-invalidating placeholders in `.github/CODEOWNERS` and `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` with concrete, repo-valid values.
+  - Rewrite remote-governance docs so inspection, diff, approval, apply, and verify are separate steps.
+  - Remove normal-workflow hook-bypass guidance from contributor docs.
+  - Make repo-settings desired state explicit and align it with actual remote settings.
+- Stronger target-state improvements:
+  - Add repo-settings desired state as a versioned table or machine-readable artifact.
+  - Add ruleset parity/drift detection and default-branch activation checks.
+  - Encode or generate the effective release bypass story instead of relying on manual UI instructions.
+  - Reframe generic environment and architecture examples as downstream-only scaffolding.
+
+## Stronger Target-State Options Not Applied
+
+- Relevant but intentionally deferred:
+  - Ruleset drift detection or sync workflow.
+  - Default-branch activation parity checks for workflows and governance.
+  - A first-class repo-settings desired-state artifact.
+- Blocked by missing org or repo prerequisites:
+  - Versioned ruleset parity that includes the real release bypass actor; that depends on concrete GitHub App or org-level identity details.
+  - Concrete `CODEOWNERS` cannot be completed truthfully without real repo owners or teams.
+
+## Release Path Viability
+
+- Current release state: viable in live remote state, but target-state governance is incomplete.
+- Chosen auth path: Octo STS token via `.github/chainguard/main-semantic-release.sts.yaml:1` and `.github/workflows/release.yml:39`.
+- Protection-model compatibility notes: live remote has no ruleset and `main` is unprotected, so releases can currently complete; if `.github/ruleset-config.json` were applied as-is, the repo would still need a documented and versioned bypass strategy for the release actor.
+- Required prerequisites not yet present: effective desired-state capture of the release bypass actor, and parity verification between local ruleset intent and live remote enforcement.
+
+## Governance Placeholder Severity
+
+- Governance-invalidating placeholders:
+  - `.github/CODEOWNERS:2`
+  - `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:5`
+  - `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:8`
+- Advisory placeholders that are acceptable for now:
+  - Template creation placeholders in `README.md:25` and workflow placeholder steps in `.github/workflows/ci.yml:28` and `.github/workflows/ci.yml:38`.
+  - Explicit architecture placeholder framing in `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:3` and `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:307`.
+- CODEOWNERS validity or enforcement notes: syntax is placeholder-only and therefore non-resolving; local ruleset intent does not require code owner review, but Renovate requests reviewers from CODEOWNERS, so the invalid placeholder still affects governance automation.
+- Unsafe direct-apply or hook-bypass guidance:
+  - Raw `gh api` apply guidance appears in `AGENTS.md:61` and `docs/template-guide.md:100`.
+  - Hook bypass guidance appears in `CONTRIBUTING.md:141` and `docs/development.md:315`.
+
+## Local Remediation Plan
+
+- Planned changes:
+  - Replace invalid ownership and issue-link placeholders with concrete values or explicit unresolved-gap text.
+  - Rewrite governance docs to lead with audit commands and approval boundaries.
+  - Remove `--no-verify` and normal `SKIP=` examples from standard contributor guidance.
+  - Mark generic environment/runtime examples as downstream customization only.
+- Applied changes: none; audit-only per request.
+- Deferred local items:
+  - Stronger target-state automation for repo-settings parity and ruleset drift detection.
+
+## Remote Governance Plan
+
+- Audited areas: repository settings, live rulesets, default-branch protection state, workflow activation, and release presence.
+- Proposed remote changes:
+  - Enable settings that match the documented desired posture if that posture is still intended.
+  - Apply a concrete ruleset only after local desired state includes the actual release bypass and ownership story.
+  - Reconcile whether issues should be enabled, since issue templates are committed.
+- Approval required before apply: yes.
+- Applied remote changes: none.
+- Deferred remote items:
+  - Any `gh api` mutation, ruleset creation, or repo-settings patching.
+
+## Validation Results
+
+- Commands run:
+  - `git remote -v`
+  - `git status --short`
+  - `gh api repos/liatrio-labs/base-repo-template`
+  - `gh api repos/liatrio-labs/base-repo-template/rulesets`
+  - `gh workflow list --repo liatrio-labs/base-repo-template`
+  - `gh api repos/liatrio-labs/base-repo-template/branches/main`
+  - `gh api repos/liatrio-labs/base-repo-template/branches/main/protection`
+  - `gh release list --repo liatrio-labs/base-repo-template`
+  - `git tag`
+  - JSON, TOML, and YAML syntax validation for the key governance/workflow files
+- Passing checks:
+  - `.github/ruleset-config.json` parses as valid JSON.
+  - `.releaserc.toml` parses as valid TOML.
+  - `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `.github/workflows/release.yml`, `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/*.yml`, `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml`, and `.github/chainguard/main-semantic-release.sts.yaml` parse as valid YAML.
+  - Remote API reads succeeded; workflows and releases are active.
+- Failing or skipped checks:
+  - `gh api .../branches/main/protection` returned `404 Branch not protected`.
+  - `pre-commit run --all-files` was intentionally skipped because this was an audit-only run and several configured hooks are mutating.
+  - Live ruleset audit returned no rulesets.
+
+## Residual Risks And Follow-Ups
+
+- The repository is close to a strong template baseline, but it is not governance-ready as-is because live ownership/support metadata is still placeholder-based and remote mutation guidance is unsafe.
+- The template-repo lens does apply, so many implementation placeholders are acceptable; the blocking issues are the ones that affect the live template repo's trust, governance, or safety boundaries.
+- The live remote currently functions because governance is weaker than the documented target. Hardening remote protections without first fixing local desired state would likely break semantic-release and still leave repo-settings drift unresolved.

- Broken issue contact links in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:3` through `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:8` are governance-invalidating placeholders, and the live remote has `has_issues=false`, so contributor support/navigation is internally inconsistent.
- Unsafe remote apply guidance is normalized in `AGENTS.md:61` through `AGENTS.md:65` and `docs/template-guide.md:100` through `docs/template-guide.md:117`; this fails the audit-first, approval-gated governance standard.

### Medium

- Hook-bypass guidance is normalized in `CONTRIBUTING.md:135` through `CONTRIBUTING.md:143` and `docs/development.md:315` through `docs/development.md:317`, including `--no-verify`, which is an explicit safety smell.
- Local governance intent and live remote state materially drift: `.github/ruleset-config.json:1` is not applied, `main` is unprotected, and live repo settings differ from the intended squash-only/delete-branch guidance in `docs/template-guide.md:100` through `docs/template-guide.md:115`.
- Release path is only viable because the live branch is currently unprotected; the versioned desired state does not encode the Octo STS bypass actor and instead relies on manual UI steps in `docs/template-guide.md:117`, so a future ruleset apply could block release pushes.
- Repo-settings desired state is not first-class or machine-readable; it is buried in raw apply prose instead of an auditable desired-state artifact.

### Low

- `docs/development.md:46` through `docs/development.md:94` mixes truthful baseline setup with generic dependency/test examples that still read close to present-tense instructions.
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:1` is clearly marked as a placeholder, but it still reads like a generic application architecture rather than a template-repo or downstream-consumer artifact.

## Minimum Baseline Vs Stronger Target State

- Minimum acceptable baseline gaps:
  - Replace governance-invalidating placeholders in `.github/CODEOWNERS` and `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` with concrete, repo-valid values.
  - Rewrite remote-governance docs so inspection, diff, approval, apply, and verify are separate steps.
  - Remove normal-workflow hook-bypass guidance from contributor docs.
  - Make repo-settings desired state explicit and align it with actual remote settings.
- Stronger target-state improvements:
  - Add repo-settings desired state as a versioned table or machine-readable artifact.
  - Add ruleset parity/drift detection and default-branch activation checks.
  - Encode or generate the effective release bypass story instead of relying on manual UI instructions.
  - Reframe generic environment and architecture examples as downstream-only scaffolding.

## Stronger Target-State Options Not Applied

- Relevant but intentionally deferred:
  - Ruleset drift detection or sync workflow.
  - Default-branch activation parity checks for workflows and governance.
  - A first-class repo-settings desired-state artifact.
- Blocked by missing org or repo prerequisites:
  - Versioned ruleset parity that includes the real release bypass actor; that depends on concrete GitHub App or org-level identity details.
  - Concrete `CODEOWNERS` cannot be completed truthfully without real repo owners or teams.

## Release Path Viability

- Current release state: viable in live remote state, but target-state governance is incomplete.
- Chosen auth path: Octo STS token via `.github/chainguard/main-semantic-release.sts.yaml:1` and `.github/workflows/release.yml:39`.
- Protection-model compatibility notes: live remote has no ruleset and `main` is unprotected, so releases can currently complete; if `.github/ruleset-config.json` were applied as-is, the repo would still need a documented and versioned bypass strategy for the release actor.
- Required prerequisites not yet present: effective desired-state capture of the release bypass actor, and parity verification between local ruleset intent and live remote enforcement.

## Governance Placeholder Severity

- Governance-invalidating placeholders:
  - `.github/CODEOWNERS:2`
  - `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:5`
  - `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml:8`
- Advisory placeholders that are acceptable for now:
  - Template creation placeholders in `README.md:25` and workflow placeholder steps in `.github/workflows/ci.yml:28` and `.github/workflows/ci.yml:38`.
  - Explicit architecture placeholder framing in `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:3` and `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:307`.
- CODEOWNERS validity or enforcement notes: syntax is placeholder-only and therefore non-resolving; local ruleset intent does not require code owner review, but Renovate requests reviewers from CODEOWNERS, so the invalid placeholder still affects governance automation.
- Unsafe direct-apply or hook-bypass guidance:
  - Raw `gh api` apply guidance appears in `AGENTS.md:61` and `docs/template-guide.md:100`.
  - Hook bypass guidance appears in `CONTRIBUTING.md:141` and `docs/development.md:315`.

## Local Remediation Plan

- Planned changes:
  - Replace invalid ownership and issue-link placeholders with concrete values or explicit unresolved-gap text.
  - Rewrite governance docs to lead with audit commands and approval boundaries.
  - Remove `--no-verify` and normal `SKIP=` examples from standard contributor guidance.
  - Mark generic environment/runtime examples as downstream customization only.
- Applied changes: none; audit-only per request.
- Deferred local items:
  - Stronger target-state automation for repo-settings parity and ruleset drift detection.

## Remote Governance Plan

- Audited areas: repository settings, live rulesets, default-branch protection state, workflow activation, and release presence.
- Proposed remote changes:
  - Enable settings that match the documented desired posture if that posture is still intended.
  - Apply a concrete ruleset only after local desired state includes the actual release bypass and ownership story.
  - Reconcile whether issues should be enabled, since issue templates are committed.
- Approval required before apply: yes.
- Applied remote changes: none.
- Deferred remote items:
  - Any `gh api` mutation, ruleset creation, or repo-settings patching.

## Validation Results

- Commands run:
  - `git remote -v`
  - `git status --short`
  - `gh api repos/liatrio-labs/base-repo-template`
  - `gh api repos/liatrio-labs/base-repo-template/rulesets`
  - `gh workflow list --repo liatrio-labs/base-repo-template`
  - `gh api repos/liatrio-labs/base-repo-template/branches/main`
  - `gh api repos/liatrio-labs/base-repo-template/branches/main/protection`
  - `gh release list --repo liatrio-labs/base-repo-template`
  - `git tag`
  - JSON, TOML, and YAML syntax validation for the key governance/workflow files
- Passing checks:
  - `.github/ruleset-config.json` parses as valid JSON.
  - `.releaserc.toml` parses as valid TOML.
  - `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `.github/workflows/release.yml`, `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/*.yml`, `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml`, and `.github/chainguard/main-semantic-release.sts.yaml` parse as valid YAML.
  - Remote API reads succeeded; workflows and releases are active.
- Failing or skipped checks:
  - `gh api .../branches/main/protection` returned `404 Branch not protected`.
  - `pre-commit run --all-files` was intentionally skipped because this was an audit-only run and several configured hooks are mutating.
  - Live ruleset audit returned no rulesets.

## Residual Risks And Follow-Ups

- The repository is close to a strong template baseline, but it is not governance-ready as-is because live ownership/support metadata is still placeholder-based and remote mutation guidance is unsafe.
- The template-repo lens does apply, so many implementation placeholders are acceptable; the blocking issues are the ones that affect the live template repo's trust, governance, or safety boundaries.
- The live remote currently functions because governance is weaker than the documented target. Hardening remote protections without first fixing local desired state would likely break semantic-release and still leave repo-settings drift unresolved.
